Among these [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range") are the high peaks of the [Fatra-Tatra Area](/wiki/Fatra-Tatra_Area "Fatra-Tatra Area") (including [Tatra Mountains](/wiki/Tatra_Mountains "Tatra Mountains"), [Greater Fatra](/wiki/Ve%C4%BEk%C3%A1_Fatra "Veľká Fatra") and [Lesser Fatra](/wiki/Lesser_Fatra "Lesser Fatra")), [Slovak Ore Mountains](/wiki/Slovak_Ore_Mountains "Slovak Ore Mountains"), [Slovak Central Mountains](/wiki/Slovak_Central_Mountains "Slovak Central Mountains") or [Beskids](/wiki/Beskids "Beskids"). The largest lowland is the fertile [Danubian Lowland](/wiki/Danubian_Lowland "Danubian Lowland") in the southwest, followed by the [Eastern Slovak Lowland](/wiki/Eastern_Slovak_Lowland "Eastern Slovak Lowland") in the southeast.[[153]](#cite_note-cia-156) Forests cover 41% of Slovak land surface.[[154]](#cite_note-157)

### Tatra mountains

Main article: [Tatra Mountains](/wiki/Tatra_Mountains "Tatra Mountains")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Slovakia_topo.jpg/500px-Slovakia_topo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slovakia_topo.jpg)

A [topographical](/wiki/Topography "Topography") map of Slovakia

The Tatra Mountains, with 29 peaks higher than 2,500 metres (8,202 feet) [AMSL](/wiki/Sea_level#AMSL "Sea level"), are the highest mountain range in the Carpathian Mountains. The Tatras occupy an area of 750 km2 (290 sq mi), of which the greater part 600 km2 (230 sq mi) lies in Slovakia. They are divided into several parts.

To the north, close to the Polish border, are the [High Tatras](/wiki/High_Tatras "High Tatras") which are a popular [hiking](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking") and [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing") destination and home to many scenic lakes and valleys as well as the highest point in Slovakia, the [Gerlachovský štít](/wiki/Gerlachovsk%C3%BD_%C5%A1t%C3%ADt "Gerlachovský štít") at 2,655 metres (8,711 ft) and the country's highly symbolic mountain [Kriváň](/wiki/Kriv%C3%A1%C5%88_(peak) "Kriváň (peak)"). To the west are the [Western Tatras](/wiki/Western_Tatras "Western Tatras") with their highest peak of [Bystrá](/wiki/Bystr%C3%A1_(mountain) "Bystrá (mountain)") at 2,248 metres (7,375 ft) and to the east are the [Belianske Tatras](/wiki/Belianske_Tatras "Belianske Tatras"), smallest by area.

Separated from the Tatras proper by the valley of the [Váh](/wiki/V%C3%A1h "Váh") river are the [Low Tatras](/wiki/Low_Tatras "Low Tatras"), with their highest peak of [Ďumbier](/wiki/%C4%8Eumbier "Ďumbier") at 2,043 metres (6,703 ft).

The Tatra mountain range is represented as one of the three hills on the [coat of arms of Slovakia](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Slovakia "Coat of arms of Slovakia").

### National parks

Main article: [List of national parks of Slovakia](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Slovakia "List of national parks of Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Palcmansk%C3%A1_Ma%C5%A1a.JPG/250px-Palcmansk%C3%A1_Ma%C5%A1a.JPG)](/wiki/File:Palcmansk%C3%A1_Ma%C5%A1a.JPG)

[Slovak Paradise National Park](/wiki/Slovak_Paradise_National_Park "Slovak Paradise National Park")

There are nine national parks in Slovakia, covering 6.5% of the Slovak land surface. These parks are: [Tatra National Park](/wiki/Tatra_National_Park,_Slovakia "Tatra National Park, Slovakia"), [Low Tatras National Park](/wiki/Low_Tatras_National_Park "Low Tatras National Park"), [Veľká Fatra National Park](/wiki/Ve%C4%BEk%C3%A1_Fatra_National_Park "Veľká Fatra National Park"), [Slovak Karst National Park](/wiki/Slovak_Karst_National_Park "Slovak Karst National Park"), [Poloniny National Park](/wiki/Poloniny_National_Park "Poloniny National Park"), [Malá Fatra National Park](/wiki/Mal%C3%A1_Fatra_National_Park "Malá Fatra National Park"), [Muránska planina National Park](/wiki/Mur%C3%A1nska_planina_National_Park "Muránska planina National Park"), [Slovak Paradise National Park](/wiki/Slovak_Paradise_National_Park "Slovak Paradise National Park") and [Pieniny National Park](/wiki/Pieniny_National_Park_(Slovakia) "Pieniny National Park (Slovakia)").[[155]](#cite_note-158)

### Caves

Main article: [List of caves in Slovakia](/wiki/List_of_caves_in_Slovakia "List of caves in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Domica_Cave_20.jpg/250px-Domica_Cave_20.jpg)](/wiki/File:Domica_Cave_20.jpg)

[Domica Cave](/wiki/Domica_Cave "Domica Cave")

Slovakia has hundreds of caves and caverns under its mountains, of which 30 are open to the public.[[156]](#cite_note-159) Most of the caves have [stalagmites](/wiki/Stalagmite "Stalagmite") rising from the ground and [stalactites](/wiki/Stalactite "Stalactite") hanging from above.

There are currently five Slovak caves under [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s World Heritage Site status: [Dobšiná Ice Cave](/wiki/Dob%C5%A1in%C3%A1_Ice_Cave "Dobšiná Ice Cave"), [Domica](/wiki/Domica "Domica"), [Gombasek Cave](/wiki/Gombasek_Cave "Gombasek Cave"), [Jasovská Cave](/wiki/Jasovsk%C3%A1_Cave "Jasovská Cave") and [Ochtinská Aragonite Cave](/wiki/Ochtinsk%C3%A1_Aragonite_Cave "Ochtinská Aragonite Cave"). Other caves open to the public include [Belianska Cave](/wiki/Belianska_Cave "Belianska Cave"), [Demänovská Cave of Liberty](/wiki/Dem%C3%A4novsk%C3%A1_Cave_of_Liberty "Demänovská Cave of Liberty"), [Demänovská Ice Cave](/wiki/Dem%C3%A4novsk%C3%A1_Ice_Cave "Demänovská Ice Cave") or [Bystrianska Cave](/wiki/Bystrianska_Cave "Bystrianska Cave").

### Rivers

Main article: [List of rivers of Slovakia](/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Slovakia "List of rivers of Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Widok_z_Trzech_Koron_w_Pieninach%2C_20231014_1446_2508.jpg/250px-Widok_z_Trzech_Koron_w_Pieninach%2C_20231014_1446_2508.jpg)](/wiki/File:Widok_z_Trzech_Koron_w_Pieninach,_20231014_1446_2508.jpg)

[Dunajec river](/wiki/Dunajec "Dunajec")

Most of the rivers arise in the Slovak mountains. Some only pass through Slovakia, while others make a natural border with surrounding countries (more than 620 kilometres [390 mi]). For example, the [Dunajec](/wiki/Dunajec "Dunajec") (17 kilometres [11 mi]) to the north, the [Danube](/wiki/Danube_River "Danube River") (172 kilometres [107 mi]) to the south or the [Morava](/wiki/Morava_(river) "Morava (river)") (119 kilometres [74 mi]) to the West. The total length of the rivers on Slovak territory is 49,774 kilometres (30,928 mi).

The longest river in Slovakia is the [Váh](/wiki/V%C3%A1h "Váh") (403 kilometres [250 mi]), the shortest is the Čierna voda. Other important and large rivers are the [Myjava](/wiki/Myjava_(river) "Myjava (river)"), the [Nitra](/wiki/Nitra_(river) "Nitra (river)") (197 kilometres [122 mi]), the [Orava](/wiki/Orava_(river) "Orava (river)"), the [Hron](/wiki/Hron "Hron") (298 kilometres [185 mi]), the [Hornád](/wiki/Horn%C3%A1d "Hornád") (193 kilometres [120 mi]), the [Slaná](/wiki/Slan%C3%A1_river "Slaná river") (110 kilometres [68 mi]), the [Ipeľ](/wiki/Ipe%C4%BE "Ipeľ") (232 kilometres [144 mi], forming the border with Hungary), the [Bodrog](/wiki/Bodrog "Bodrog"), the [Laborec](/wiki/Laborec "Laborec"), the [Latorica](/wiki/Latorica "Latorica") and the [Ondava](/wiki/Ondava "Ondava").

The biggest volume of discharge in Slovak rivers is during [spring](/wiki/Spring_(season) "Spring (season)"), when the snow melts from the mountains. The only exception is the Danube, whose discharge is the greatest during summer when the snow melts in the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"). The Danube is the largest river that flows through Slovakia.[[157]](#cite_note-160)

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/1._m%C3%A1j_Hlavn%C3%A1_ulica_Pre%C5%A1ov_23_Slovakia_38.jpg/250px-1._m%C3%A1j_Hlavn%C3%A1_ulica_Pre%C5%A1ov_23_Slovakia_38.jpg)](/wiki/File:1._m%C3%A1j_Hlavn%C3%A1_ulica_Pre%C5%A1ov_23_Slovakia_38.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/CulHer14Slovakia328.JPG/250px-CulHer14Slovakia328.JPG)](/wiki/File:CulHer14Slovakia328.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/DanielovDomVysok%C3%A9Tatry15Slovakia31.JPG/250px-DanielovDomVysok%C3%A9Tatry15Slovakia31.JPG)](/wiki/File:DanielovDomVysok%C3%A9Tatry15Slovakia31.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Bansk%C3%A1_%C5%A0tiavnica_im_Herbst.JPG/250px-Bansk%C3%A1_%C5%A0tiavnica_im_Herbst.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bansk%C3%A1_%C5%A0tiavnica_im_Herbst.JPG)

Four seasons in Slovakia: spring in [Prešov](/wiki/Pre%C5%A1ov "Prešov"), summer in [Spišské Podhradie](/wiki/Spi%C5%A1sk%C3%A9_Podhradie "Spišské Podhradie"), autumn in [High Tatras](/wiki/High_Tatras "High Tatras"), winter in [Banská Štiavnica](/wiki/Bansk%C3%A1_%C5%A0tiavnica "Banská Štiavnica") (the town is a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site"))

The Slovak climate lies between the temperate and [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") zones with relatively warm [summers](/wiki/Summer "Summer") and cold, cloudy and humid [winters](/wiki/Winter "Winter"). Temperature extremes are between −41 and 40.3 °C (−41.8 and 104.5 °F) although temperatures below −30 °C (−22 °F) are rare. The weather differs from the mountainous north to the plains in the south.

The warmest region is [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava") and Southern Slovakia where the temperatures may reach 30 °C (86 °F) in summer, occasionally to 39 °C (102 °F) in [Hurbanovo](/wiki/Hurbanovo "Hurbanovo"). During night, the temperatures drop to 20 °C (68 °F). The daily temperatures in winter average in the range of −5 °C (23 °F) to 10 °C (50 °F). During night it may be freezing, but usually not below −10 °C (14 °F).

In Slovakia, there are four [seasons](/wiki/Season "Season"), each season ([spring](/wiki/Spring_(season) "Spring (season)"), summer, [autumn](/wiki/Autumn "Autumn") and winter) lasts three months. The dry continental air brings in the summer heat and winter frosts. In contrast, oceanic air brings rainfalls and reduces summer temperatures. In the lowlands and valleys, there is often fog, especially in winter.

Spring starts with 21 March and is characterised by colder weather with an average daily temperature of 9 °C (48 °F) in the first weeks and about 14 °C (57 °F) in May and 17 °C (63 °F) in June. In Slovakia, the weather and climate in the spring are very unstable.

Summer starts on 22 June and is usually characterised by hot weather with daily temperatures exceeding 30 °C (86 °F). July is the warmest month with temperatures up to about 37 to 40 °C (99 to 104 °F), especially in regions of southern Slovakia. Showers or thunderstorms may occur because of the summer monsoon called Medardova kvapka (Medard drop – 40 days of rain). Summer in Northern Slovakia is usually mild with temperatures around 25 °C (77 °F) (less in the mountains).

Autumn in Slovakia starts on 23 September and is mostly characterised by wet weather and wind, although the first weeks can be very warm and sunny. The average temperature in September is around 14 °C (57 °F), in November to 3 °C (37 °F). Late September and early October is a dry and sunny time of year (so-called [Indian summer](/wiki/Indian_summer "Indian summer")).

Winter starts on 21 December with temperatures around −5 to −10 °C (23 to 14 °F). In December and January, it is usually snowing, these are the coldest months of the year. At lower altitudes, snow does not stay the whole winter, it changes into the thaw and frost. Winters are colder in the mountains, where the snow usually lasts until March or April and the night temperatures fall to −20 °C (−4 °F) and colder.[[158]](#cite_note-161)

### Biodiversity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Vysok%C3%A9_Tatry%2C_Dolina_Bielej_vody%2C_cestou_od_Zelen%C3%A9ho_plesa_na_Jah%C5%88ac%C3%AD_%C5%A1t%C3%ADt_%2832%29.JPG/250px-Vysok%C3%A9_Tatry%2C_Dolina_Bielej_vody%2C_cestou_od_Zelen%C3%A9ho_plesa_na_Jah%C5%88ac%C3%AD_%C5%A1t%C3%ADt_%2832%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vysok%C3%A9_Tatry,_Dolina_Bielej_vody,_cestou_od_Zelen%C3%A9ho_plesa_na_Jah%C5%88ac%C3%AD_%C5%A1t%C3%ADt_(32).JPG)

*[Rupicapra rupicapra tatrica](/wiki/Rupicapra_rupicapra_tatrica "Rupicapra rupicapra tatrica")* in the [Tatra Mountains](/wiki/Tatra_Mountains "Tatra Mountains")

Slovakia signed the Rio [Convention on Biological Diversity](/wiki/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity "Convention on Biological Diversity") on 19 May 1993, and became a party to the convention on 25 August 1994.[[159]](#cite_note-162) It has subsequently produced a [National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan](/wiki/Biodiversity_action_plan "Biodiversity action plan"), which was received by the convention on 2 November 1998.[[160]](#cite_note-163)

The biodiversity of Slovakia comprises [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal") (such as annelids, arthropods, molluscs, nematodes and vertebrates), [fungi](/wiki/Fungi "Fungi") ([Ascomycota](/wiki/Ascomycota "Ascomycota"), [Basidiomycota](/wiki/Basidiomycota "Basidiomycota"), [Chytridiomycota](/wiki/Chytridiomycota "Chytridiomycota"), [Glomeromycota](/wiki/Glomeromycota "Glomeromycota") and [Zygomycota](/wiki/Zygomycota "Zygomycota")), micro-organisms (including [Mycetozoa](/wiki/Mycetozoa "Mycetozoa")), and [plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant"). The geographical position of Slovakia determines the richness of the diversity of fauna and flora. More than 11,000 plant species have been described throughout its territory, nearly 29,000 animal species and over 1,000 species of protozoa. [Endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") biodiversity is also common.[[161]](#cite_note-geography.upol.cz-164)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Belianske_Tatry.jpg/250px-Belianske_Tatry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belianske_Tatry.jpg)

[Belianske Tatras](/wiki/Belianske_Tatras "Belianske Tatras")

Slovakia is located in the biome of [temperate broadleaf and mixed forests](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_and_mixed_forest "Temperate broadleaf and mixed forest") and terrestrial ecoregions of [Pannonian mixed forests](/wiki/Pannonian_mixed_forests "Pannonian mixed forests") and [Carpathian montane conifer forests](/wiki/Carpathian_montane_conifer_forests "Carpathian montane conifer forests").[[162]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-165) As the altitude changes, the vegetation associations and animal communities are forming height levels ([oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), [beech](/wiki/Beech "Beech"), [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce"), [scrub pine](/wiki/Scrub_pine "Scrub pine"), [alpine meadows](/wiki/Alpine_meadows "Alpine meadows") and [subsoil](/wiki/Subsoil "Subsoil")). Forests cover 44% of the territory of Slovakia.[[163]](#cite_note-166) The country had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 4.34/10, ranking it 129th globally out of 172 countries.[[164]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-167) In terms of forest stands, 60% are [broadleaf trees](/wiki/Broadleaf_trees "Broadleaf trees") and 40% are [coniferous trees](/wiki/Coniferous_trees "Coniferous trees"). The occurrence of animal species is strongly connected to the appropriate types of plant associations and biotopes.[[161]](#cite_note-geography.upol.cz-164)

Over 4,000 species of fungi have been recorded from Slovakia.[[165]](#cite_note-168)[[166]](#cite_note-169) Of these, nearly 1,500 are [lichen](/wiki/Lichen "Lichen")-forming species.[[167]](#cite_note-170) Some of these fungi are undoubtedly endemic, but not enough is known to say how many. Of the lichen-forming species, about 40% have been classified as threatened in some way. About 7% are apparently extinct, 9% endangered, 17% vulnerable, and 7% rare. The conservation status of non-lichen-forming fungi in Slovakia is not well documented, but there is a red list for its larger fungi.[[168]](#cite_note-171)

### Water

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Mountain_lake_%28Unsplash%29.jpg/250px-Mountain_lake_%28Unsplash%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mountain_lake_(Unsplash).jpg)

Mountain lake [Štrbské pleso](/wiki/%C5%A0trbsk%C3%A9_pleso "Štrbské pleso")

The entire population of Slovakia has access to a safe-drinking water source.[[169]](#cite_note-172) The country has one of the best quality tap water in the world, it is the second country in Europe (after [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria")) with the largest reserves of drinking water. Groundwater is the drinking water source of the highest quality protected by [Constitution of Slovakia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovakia "Constitution of Slovakia"). Since 2014, it is banned the export of [drinking](/wiki/Drinking_water "Drinking water") and [mineral waters](/wiki/Mineral_water "Mineral water") in pipelines and water tanks. The ban excludes bottled water and water for personal use. Both, groundwater (82.2%) as well as surface water (17.8%) are exploited as drinking water sources. [Žitný ostrov](/wiki/%C5%BDitn%C3%BD_ostrov "Žitný ostrov") is the biggest natural groundwater source in Slovakia and as well in Central Europe.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

About 1300 mineral sources are registered, providing curative water and high quality [mineral water](/wiki/Mineral_water "Mineral water") for drinking. There are 21 thermal [spa towns](/wiki/Spa_town "Spa town") built on these mineral springs. The most visited are [Piešťany](/wiki/Pie%C5%A1%C5%A5any "Piešťany"), [Trenčianske Teplice](/wiki/Tren%C4%8Dianske_Teplice "Trenčianske Teplice"), [Bardejov](/wiki/Bardejov "Bardejov") and [Dudince](/wiki/Dudince "Dudince").

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Politics of Slovakia](/wiki/Politics_of_Slovakia "Politics of Slovakia") and [Law of Slovakia](/wiki/Law_of_Slovakia "Law of Slovakia")

See also: [Prime Minister of Slovakia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovakia "Prime Minister of Slovakia"), [President of Slovakia](/wiki/President_of_Slovakia "President of Slovakia"), and [National Council of the Slovak Republic](/wiki/National_Council_of_the_Slovak_Republic "National Council of the Slovak Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Robert_Fico%2C_Nov_2023.jpg/250px-Robert_Fico%2C_Nov_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Fico,_Nov_2023.jpg)

[Robert Fico](/wiki/Robert_Fico "Robert Fico"),  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovakia "Prime Minister of Slovakia")  
since 2023

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Peter_Pellegrini%2C_2024.jpg/250px-Peter_Pellegrini%2C_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peter_Pellegrini,_2024.jpg)

[Peter Pellegrini](/wiki/Peter_Pellegrini "Peter Pellegrini"),  
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Flag_of_the_President_of_Slovakia.svg/20px-Flag_of_the_President_of_Slovakia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_President_of_Slovakia.svg) [President](/wiki/President_of_Slovakia "President of Slovakia")  
since 2024

Slovakia is a [parliamentary democratic](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") republic with a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). The last [parliamentary elections](/wiki/2023_Slovak_parliamentary_election "2023 Slovak parliamentary election") were held on 30 September 2023 and two rounds of [presidential elections](/wiki/2024_Slovak_presidential_election "2024 Slovak presidential election") took place on 23 March and 6 April 2024.

The Slovak [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and the formal head of the executive is the president (currently [Peter Pellegrini](/wiki/Peter_Pellegrini "Peter Pellegrini")), though with very limited powers. The president is elected by direct, popular vote under the [two-round system](/wiki/Two-round_system "Two-round system") for a five-year term. Most [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") power lies with the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), the prime minister (currently [Robert Fico](/wiki/Robert_Fico "Robert Fico")), who is usually the leader of the winning party and who needs to form a majority coalition in the parliament. The prime minister is appointed by the president. The remainder of the cabinet is appointed by the president on the recommendation of the prime minister.

Slovakia's highest [legislative](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") body is the 150-seat [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") [National Council of the Slovak Republic](/wiki/National_Council_(Slovakia) "National Council (Slovakia)") (*Národná rada Slovenskej republiky*). Delegates are elected for a four-year term on the basis of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").

Slovakia's highest [judicial body](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") is the [Constitutional Court of Slovakia](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Slovakia "Constitutional Court of Slovakia") (*Ústavný súd*), which rules on constitutional issues. The 13 members of this court are appointed by the president from a slate of candidates nominated by parliament.

The [Constitution of the Slovak Republic](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovakia "Constitution of Slovakia") was ratified 1 September 1992, and became effective 1 January 1993. It was amended in September 1998 to allow [direct election](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election") of the president and again in February 2001 due to EU admission requirements. The [civil law system](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") is based on Austro-Hungarian codes. The [legal code](/wiki/Code_of_law "Code of law") was modified to comply with the obligations of [Organisation on Security and Cooperation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") (OSCE) and to expunge the [Marxist–Leninist](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") [legal theory](/wiki/Law "Law"). Slovakia accepts the compulsory [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") jurisdiction with reservations.

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Slovakia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Slovakia "Foreign relations of Slovakia")

See also: [Minister of Foreign and European Affairs](/wiki/Minister_of_Foreign_and_European_Affairs "Minister of Foreign and European Affairs") and [List of diplomatic missions of Slovakia](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_Slovakia "List of diplomatic missions of Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/President_Trump_Welcomes_the_Prime_Minister_of_the_Slovak_Republic_to_the_White_House_%2833889395928%29.jpg/250px-President_Trump_Welcomes_the_Prime_Minister_of_the_Slovak_Republic_to_the_White_House_%2833889395928%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Trump_Welcomes_the_Prime_Minister_of_the_Slovak_Republic_to_the_White_House_(33889395928).jpg)

Former Slovak Prime Minister [Peter Pellegrini](/wiki/Peter_Pellegrini "Peter Pellegrini") (current President of Slovakia) with U.S. President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") in the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), 2019

The [Ministry of Foreign and European Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Slovakia) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Slovakia)") ([Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"): *Ministerstvo zahraničných vecí a európskych záležitostí*) is responsible for maintaining the Slovak Republic's [external relations](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Slovakia "Foreign relations of Slovakia") and the management of its [international diplomatic missions](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_Slovakia "List of diplomatic missions of Slovakia"). The ministry's [director](/wiki/Minister_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Slovakia) "Minister of Foreign Affairs (Slovakia)") is [Juraj Blanár](/wiki/Juraj_Blan%C3%A1r "Juraj Blanár"). The ministry oversees Slovakia's affairs with foreign entities, including bilateral relations with individual nations and its representation in international organisations.

Slovakia joined the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") in 2004, the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area") in 2007 and the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") in 2009.

Slovakia is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (since 1993) and participates in its specialised agencies. The country was, on 10 October 2005, elected to a two-year term on the [UN Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") from 2006 to 2007. It is also a member of the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") (CoE), the [Organisation for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") (OSCE), the [World Trade Organisation](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO), the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO), the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/OECD "OECD") (OECD), the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF), the [Union for the Mediterranean](/wiki/Union_for_the_Mediterranean "Union for the Mediterranean") (UfM), the [International Criminal Police Organisation](/wiki/International_Criminal_Police_Organization "International Criminal Police Organization") (INTERPOL), the [United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation](/wiki/United_Nations_Educational,_Scientific_and_Cultural_Organization "United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization") (UNESCO), the [European Organisation for Nuclear Research](/wiki/European_Organization_for_Nuclear_Research "European Organization for Nuclear Research") (CERN), the [Bucharest Nine](/wiki/Bucharest_Nine "Bucharest Nine") (B9) and part of the [Visegrád Group](/wiki/Visegr%C3%A1d_Group "Visegrád Group") (V4: Slovakia, Hungary, the Czech Republic, and Poland).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/President_Putin_meeting_PM_of_Slovakia_Robert_Fico_%282025%29.jpg/250px-President_Putin_meeting_PM_of_Slovakia_Robert_Fico_%282025%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Putin_meeting_PM_of_Slovakia_Robert_Fico_(2025).jpg)

Slovak Prime Minister [Robert Fico](/wiki/Robert_Fico "Robert Fico") with Russian President [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), 2025

In 2025, Slovak citizens had visa-free or visa-on-arrival access to 185 countries and territories, putting the [Slovak passport](/wiki/Slovak_passport "Slovak passport") at the 6th rank of travel freedom (tied with Hungary, Malta, New Zealand, Poland and Slovenia) on the [Henley Passport Index](/wiki/Henley_Passport_Index "Henley Passport Index").[[173]](#cite_note-176)

Slovakia maintains [diplomatic relations](/wiki/Diplomacy "Diplomacy") with 134 countries, primarily through its [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Slovakia) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Slovakia)"). Slovakia maintained 90 missions abroad, including 64 embassies, seven missions to multilateral organisations, nine consulates-general, one consular office, one Slovak Economic and Cultural Office and eight Slovak Institutes.[[174]](#cite_note-177) Bratislava hosts 41 foreign [embassies](/wiki/Diplomatic_mission "Diplomatic mission") and 22 [consulates](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)").[[175]](#cite_note-178)

Largest trading partners are countries of the European Union. In 2024, more than 80% of Slovak exports went to the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), and more than 65% of Slovak imports came from other European Union member states.[[176]](#cite_note-Statistical_Office_of_the_Slovak_Republic-2024-179)

Slovakia and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") retain strong diplomatic ties and cooperate in the [military](/wiki/Military "Military") and [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement_agency "Law enforcement agency") areas. U.S. Department of Defence programmes has contributed significantly to Slovak military reforms. The two countries have a long history tracing back to the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), when a Slovak Major, Ján Ladislav Polerecký, fought alongside [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") in [Yorktowne](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown "Siege of Yorktown") to win the colonies' independence.[[177]](#cite_note-180)
President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") and the United States played a major role in the establishment of the original [Czechoslovak](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic") state on 28 October 1918. Around one million Americans have their roots in Slovakia, and many retain strong cultural and familial ties to the Slovak Republic.

### Military

Main article: [Slovak Armed Forces](/wiki/Slovak_Armed_Forces "Slovak Armed Forces")

See also: [Slovak Air Force](/wiki/Slovak_Air_Force "Slovak Air Force")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/UNFICYP_peacekeepers_patrolling_the_buffer_zone.jpg/250px-UNFICYP_peacekeepers_patrolling_the_buffer_zone.jpg)](/wiki/File:UNFICYP_peacekeepers_patrolling_the_buffer_zone.jpg)

Slovak members of [UNFICYP](/wiki/UNFICYP "UNFICYP") peacekeepers patrolling the [buffer zone](/wiki/United_Nations_Buffer_Zone_in_Cyprus "United Nations Buffer Zone in Cyprus") in Cyprus

The president is formally the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the Slovak armed forces.

Slovakia joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") in March 2004.[[178]](#cite_note-181) From 2006, the army transformed into a fully professional organisation and compulsory military service was abolished. Slovak armed forces numbered 19,500 uniformed personnel and 4,208 civilians in 2022.[[179]](#cite_note-182)

As of 2025, Slovakia has 240 military personnel deployed in [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") for [UNFICYP](/wiki/UNFICYP "UNFICYP") [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") led [peace support operations](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping"), 50 troops deployed in [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") for [EUFOR Althea](/wiki/EUFOR_Althea "EUFOR Althea") and 135 troops deployed in [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") for [NATO Enhanced Forward Presence](/wiki/NATO_Enhanced_Forward_Presence "NATO Enhanced Forward Presence").[[180]](#cite_note-183)

The Slovak [Ground Forces](/wiki/Ground_Forces_of_the_Slovak_Republic "Ground Forces of the Slovak Republic") are made up of two active [mechanised infantry](/wiki/Mechanised_infantry "Mechanised infantry") [brigades](/wiki/Brigade "Brigade"). The [Air and Air Defence Forces](/wiki/Slovak_Air_Force "Slovak Air Force") comprise one [wing](/wiki/Wing_(military_unit) "Wing (military unit)") of [fighters](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft"), one wing of utility [helicopters](/wiki/Helicopter "Helicopter"), and one SAM brigade. Training and support forces comprise a National Support Element (Multifunctional Battalion, Transport Battalion, Repair Battalion), a garrison force of the capital city Bratislava, as well as a training battalion, and various logistics, communication, and information bases. Miscellaneous forces under the direct command of the [General Staff](/wiki/Staff_(military) "Staff (military)") include the [5th Special Forces Regiment](/wiki/5th_Special_Operations_Regiment_(Slovakia) "5th Special Operations Regiment (Slovakia)").

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in Slovakia](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Slovakia "Human rights in Slovakia")

Human rights in Slovakia are guaranteed by the [Constitution of Slovakia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovakia "Constitution of Slovakia") from the year 1992 and by multiple international laws signed in Slovakia between 1948 and 2006.[[181]](#cite_note-184) Slovakia performs favourably in measurements of [civil liberties](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World"), [press freedom](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index"), [internet freedom](/wiki/Internet_censorship_and_surveillance_by_country "Internet censorship and surveillance by country"), [democratic governance](/wiki/Democracy_Index "Democracy Index"), and [peacefulness](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index").

[The US State Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") in 2021 reported:

> The government generally respected the human rights of its citizens; however, there were problems in some areas. Significant human rights issues included credible reports of: corruption; violence and threats of violence against Roma and members of other ethnic and racial minorities; and violence and threats of violence targeting lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, and intersex persons.[[182]](#cite_note-185)

According to the [European Roma Rights Centre](/wiki/European_Roma_Rights_Centre "European Roma Rights Centre") (ERRC), [Romani people in Slovakia](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Slovakia "Romani people in Slovakia") "endure racism in the job market, housing and education fields and are often subjected to forced evictions, vigilante intimidation, disproportionate levels of police brutality and more subtle forms of discrimination."[[183]](#cite_note-186)

According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance")’s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Slovakia performs in the mid-range on overall democratic measures, with particular strengths in political equality and judicial independence.[[184]](#cite_note-187)[[185]](#cite_note-188)[[186]](#cite_note-189)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Regions of Slovakia](/wiki/Regions_of_Slovakia "Regions of Slovakia"), [Districts of Slovakia](/wiki/Districts_of_Slovakia "Districts of Slovakia"), and [List of municipalities and towns in Slovakia](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_and_towns_in_Slovakia "List of municipalities and towns in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Hlavn%C3%A9_Namestie_%2835096533142%29.jpg/250px-Hlavn%C3%A9_Namestie_%2835096533142%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hlavn%C3%A9_Namestie_(35096533142).jpg)

[Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava"), capital and largest city of Slovakia

Slovakia is divided into 8 *[kraje](/wiki/Kraj "Kraj")* (singular—*kraj*, usually translated as "region"), each of which is named after its principal city. Regions have enjoyed a certain degree of autonomy since 2002. Their [self-governing](/wiki/Self-governance "Self-governance") bodies are referred to as Self-governing (or autonomous) Regions (sg. *samosprávny kraj*, pl. *samosprávne kraje*) or Upper-Tier Territorial Units (sg. *vyšší územný celok*, pl. *vyššie územné celky*, abbr. VÚC).

The *kraje* are subdivided into *[okresy](/wiki/Okres "Okres")* (sg. *okres*, usually translated as districts). Slovakia currently has 79 districts.

The *okresy* are further divided into *[obce](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_and_towns_in_Slovakia "List of municipalities and towns in Slovakia")* (sg. *obec*, usually translated as "municipality"). There are currently 2,890 municipalities.

In terms of economics and [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") rate, the western regions are richer than eastern regions. Bratislava is the 19th-richest region of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") by GDP (PPP) per capita;[[187]](#cite_note-190) GDP at purchasing power parity is about three times higher than in other Slovak regions.[[188]](#cite_note-finweb.hnonline.sk-191)[[189]](#cite_note-192)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Slovakia](/wiki/Economy_of_Slovakia "Economy of Slovakia")

See also: [Slovak euro coins](/wiki/Slovak_euro_coins "Slovak euro coins")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Nbs.jpg/250px-Nbs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nbs.jpg)

[National Bank of Slovakia](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Slovakia "National Bank of Slovakia") in Bratislava

Slovakia has a [high-income](/wiki/High-income_country "High-income country") [developed economy](/wiki/Developed_economies "Developed economies"). In 2026, with a population of only 5 million, it ranked as the [47th richest country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") with a [per capita gross domestic product](/wiki/Per_capita_GDP "Per capita GDP") based on [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") of $49,376,[[190]](#cite_note-193) and the [58th largest economy in the World](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") with a GDP of $167,730 billion.[[191]](#cite_note-194) Its GDP per capita equalling 74% of the average of the European Union in 2023.[[192]](#cite_note-rich-195) Major [privatisations](/wiki/Privatisation "Privatisation") are completed, the banking sector is almost completely in private hands, and foreign investment has risen.

In 2024, more than 80% of Slovak exports went to the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), and more than 65% of Slovak imports came from other European Union member states.[[193]](#cite_note-196)[[176]](#cite_note-Statistical_Office_of_the_Slovak_Republic-2024-179) Main export partners are Germany (23% of total exports), Czech Republic (12.4%), Poland (8.3%) and Austria (5.7%).

The country has difficulties addressing regional imbalances in wealth and employment.[[194]](#cite_note-197) GDP per capita ranges from 188% of EU average in Bratislava to 54% in Eastern Slovakia.[[195]](#cite_note-198) Bratislava is the 19th-richest region of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") by GDP ([PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")) per capita.[[196]](#cite_note-199) Although regional income inequality is high, [90% of citizens own their homes](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_home_ownership_rate "List of countries by home ownership rate").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Eurozone.svg/250px-Eurozone.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Eurozone.svg)

Slovakia is part of the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area"), the EU single market, and since 2009, the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") (dark blue).

The country used to be dubbed the *"[Tatra Tiger](/wiki/Tatra_Tiger "Tatra Tiger")"* in the 2000s as it successfully transformed from a centrally [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") to a market-driven economy and achieved, on average, roughly 6% per capita GDP growth each year from 2000 to 2008.[[102]](#cite_note-Nicolae-Dragos-2015-105)[[103]](#cite_note-Akadémiai_Kiadó-106)

Unemployment, peaking at 19% at the end of 1999, decreased to 4.9% in 2019, lowest recorded rate in Slovak history.[[197]](#cite_note-200) Unemployment rate was 5.3% as per June 2025.[[198]](#cite_note-201)[[199]](#cite_note-202)

The ratio of government debt to GDP in Slovakia reached 60.5% in 2024.[[200]](#cite_note-203)

A high dependence on global supply chains and energy imports makes the Slovak economy vulnerable to external shocks. As a consequence, the economy was hit hard by the global [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") crisis (−3.3% decline in 2020), despite sizeable economic policy support. And after a strong recovery in 2021 (+4.8% growth), growth slowed down markedly in 2022 (+1.9%) and 2023 (+1.6%) as a result of the consequences of the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), notably the subsequent [EU sanctions on Russia](/wiki/International_sanctions_during_the_Russo-Ukrainian_War "International sanctions during the Russo-Ukrainian War") and soaring energy prices.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Sky_Park_Bratislava.jpg/250px-Sky_Park_Bratislava.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sky_Park_Bratislava.jpg)

High-rise buildings in Bratislava's business districts

The Slovak government encourages foreign investment since it is one of the driving forces of the economy. Slovakia is an attractive country for [foreign investors](/wiki/Investment_(macroeconomics) "Investment (macroeconomics)") mainly because of its low wages, low tax rates, well educated [labour force](/wiki/Labour_force "Labour force"), favourable geographic location in the heart of Central Europe, strong political stability and good international relations reinforced by the country's accession to the European Union. Some regions, mostly at the east of Slovakia have failed to attract major investment, which has aggravated regional disparities in many economic and social areas.

Slovakia adopted the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") currency on 1 January 2009 as the 16th member of the eurozone. The euro in Slovakia was approved by [the European commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") on 7 May 2008. The [Slovak koruna](/wiki/Slovak_koruna "Slovak koruna") was revalued on 28 May 2008 to 30.126 for 1 euro, which was also the exchange rate for the euro.[[201]](#cite_note-204)[[202]](#cite_note-205)

Slovakia ranks 45th out of 190 economies in terms of ease of doing business, according to the [2020 World Bank Doing Business Report](/wiki/Ease_of_doing_business_index "Ease of doing business index") and 59th out of the 67 countries and territories in terms of competitive economy, according to the [2024 World Competitiveness Yearbook Report](/wiki/World_Competitiveness_Yearbook "World Competitiveness Yearbook").

### Industry

See also: [Automotive industry in Slovakia](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Slovakia "Automotive industry in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Kia-factory-slovakia.jpg/250px-Kia-factory-slovakia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kia-factory-slovakia.jpg)

[Kia](/wiki/Kia "Kia")'s car production plant in [Žilina](/wiki/%C5%BDilina "Žilina")

Although Slovakia's GDP comes mainly from the tertiary (services) sector, the industrial sector also plays an important role within its economy. The main industry sectors are [car manufacturing](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Slovakia "Automotive industry in Slovakia") and [electrical engineering](/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering"). Since 2007, Slovakia has been the world's largest producer of cars per capita,[[203]](#cite_note-Kia-206) with a total of 1,080,000 cars manufactured in the country in 2023 alone.[[18]](#cite_note-spectator.sme.sk-21) 275,000 people are employed directly and indirectly
by the automotive industry.[[204]](#cite_note-sario.sk-207) There are currently four automobile assembly plants, with a fifth under construction: [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen")'s in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava") (models: [Volkswagen Up](/wiki/Volkswagen_Up "Volkswagen Up"), [Volkswagen Touareg](/wiki/Volkswagen_Touareg "Volkswagen Touareg"), [Audi Q7](/wiki/Audi_Q7 "Audi Q7"), [Audi Q8](/wiki/Audi_Q8 "Audi Q8"), [Porsche Cayenne](/wiki/Porsche_Cayenne "Porsche Cayenne"), [Lamborghini Urus](/wiki/Lamborghini_Urus "Lamborghini Urus")), [PSA Peugeot Citroën](/wiki/PSA_Peugeot_Citro%C3%ABn "PSA Peugeot Citroën")'s in [Trnava](/wiki/Trnava "Trnava") (models: [Peugeot 208](/wiki/Peugeot_208 "Peugeot 208"), [Citroën C3 Picasso](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_C3_Picasso "Citroën C3 Picasso")), [Kia Motors](/wiki/Kia_Motors "Kia Motors")' [Žilina Plant](/wiki/Kia_Design_and_Manufacturing_Facilities#Žilina_Plant "Kia Design and Manufacturing Facilities") (models: [Kia Cee'd](/wiki/Kia_Cee%27d "Kia Cee'd"), [Kia Sportage](/wiki/Kia_Sportage "Kia Sportage"), [Kia Venga](/wiki/Kia_Venga "Kia Venga")) and [Jaguar Land Rover](/wiki/Jaguar_Land_Rover "Jaguar Land Rover")'s in [Nitra](/wiki/Nitra "Nitra") (model: [Land Rover Defender](/wiki/Land_Rover_Defender "Land Rover Defender"), [Land Rover Discovery](/wiki/Land_Rover_Discovery#Third_generation_(L462;_2017–present) "Land Rover Discovery")). [Volvo](/wiki/Volvo "Volvo") will make electric cars at a new plant at the east of Slovakia, construction began in 2023 in [Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice"), with series production starting in 2027.[[205]](#cite_note-208) [Hyundai Mobis](/wiki/Hyundai_Mobis "Hyundai Mobis") in [Žilina](/wiki/%C5%BDilina "Žilina") is the largest suppliers for the automotive industry in Slovakia.[[206]](#cite_note-etrend.sk-209)

[U.S. Steel](/wiki/U._S._Steel_Ko%C5%A1ice "U. S. Steel Košice") in [Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice"), a branch of the American steel producer [U.S. Steel](/wiki/U.S._Steel "U.S. Steel"), is the largest employer in eastern Slovakia with 7,500 employees.[[207]](#cite_note-210)

[ESET](/wiki/ESET "ESET") is an IT security company from Bratislava with more than 1,000[[208]](#cite_note-211) employees worldwide at present. Their branch offices are in the United States, Ireland, United Kingdom, Argentina, the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and Poland.[[209]](#cite_note-212) In recent years, [service](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") and [high-tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech")-oriented businesses have prospered in Bratislava. Many global companies, including [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell"), [Lenovo](/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo"), [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T"), [SAP](/wiki/SAP_AG "SAP AG"), and [Accenture](/wiki/Accenture "Accenture"), have built [outsourcing](/wiki/Outsourcing "Outsourcing") and service centres here.[[210]](#cite_note-213) Reasons for the influx of [multi-national corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") include proximity to Western Europe, skilled labour force and the high density of universities and research facilities.[[211]](#cite_note-214) Other large companies and employers with headquarters in Bratislava include [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)"), [Slovak Telekom](/wiki/Slovak_Telekom "Slovak Telekom"), [Orange Slovensko](/wiki/Orange_Slovensko "Orange Slovensko"), [Slovenská sporiteľňa](/wiki/Slovensk%C3%A1_sporite%C4%BE%C5%88a "Slovenská sporiteľňa"), [Tatra banka](/wiki/Tatra_banka "Tatra banka"), [Doprastav](/wiki/Doprastav "Doprastav"), [Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard") Slovakia, [Henkel](/wiki/Henkel "Henkel") Slovensko, [Slovenský plynárenský priemysel](/wiki/Slovensk%C3%BD_plyn%C3%A1rensk%C3%BD_priemysel "Slovenský plynárenský priemysel"), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") Slovakia, [Mondelez Slovakia](/wiki/Mondelez_International "Mondelez International"), [Whirlpool Slovakia](/wiki/Whirlpool_Corporation "Whirlpool Corporation") and [Zurich Insurance Group](/wiki/Zurich_Insurance_Group "Zurich Insurance Group") Slovakia.

Bratislava's geographical position in Central Europe has long made Bratislava a crossroads for [international trade](/wiki/International_trade "International trade") traffic.[[212]](#cite_note-215)[[213]](#cite_note-216) Various ancient [trade routes](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route"), such as the [Amber Road](/wiki/Amber_Road "Amber Road") and the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") waterway, have crossed territory of present-day Bratislava. Today, Bratislava is a road, railway, waterway and airway hub.[[214]](#cite_note-transport-217)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Slovakia](/wiki/Energy_in_Slovakia "Energy in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Jaslovske_Bohunice_Power_Plant_1.JPG/250px-Jaslovske_Bohunice_Power_Plant_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jaslovske_Bohunice_Power_Plant_1.JPG)

Nuclear Power Plant [Jaslovské Bohunice](/wiki/Bohunice_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Bohunice Nuclear Power Plant")

Slovakia is self-sufficient in electricity production following the launch of the third unit of the Mochovce nuclear power plant in 2023.[[215]](#cite_note-218)

In 2020, Slovakia produced a total of 29,322 [GWh](/wiki/GWh "GWh") of [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity"). [Nuclear energy](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") accounted for 54% (15 400 GWh) of total electricity production, followed by 16% by [hydro power](/wiki/Hydro_power "Hydro power") energy, 16% natural gas, 8% coal, biofuel 3% and 3% by [solar energy](/wiki/Solar_energy "Solar energy"),[[216]](#cite_note-worl823-219)[[217]](#cite_note-220)

The two nuclear power-plants in Slovakia are in [Jaslovské Bohunice](/wiki/Jaslovsk%C3%A9_Bohunice_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Jaslovské Bohunice Nuclear Power Plant") and [Mochovce](/wiki/Mochovce_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Mochovce Nuclear Power Plant"). Jaslovské Bohunice containing two operating reactors. Mochovce containing three operating reactors, unit Mochovce-3 came on-line in January 2023 and unit Mochovce-4 will be completed at the end of 2025.[[216]](#cite_note-worl823-219) The additional reactors in Mochovce returned Slovakia to being a net exporter of electricity.[[218]](#cite_note-221)[[219]](#cite_note-222)

In 2024, Slovak government approved a plan to build another new nuclear reactor in [Jaslovské Bohunice](/wiki/Jaslovsk%C3%A9_Bohunice_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Jaslovské Bohunice Nuclear Power Plant").[[220]](#cite_note-223)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Refinery_of_Slovnaft%2C_view_from_Nov%C3%BD_most_viewpoint_in_Bratislava%2C_Bratislava_II_District.jpg/250px-Refinery_of_Slovnaft%2C_view_from_Nov%C3%BD_most_viewpoint_in_Bratislava%2C_Bratislava_II_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Refinery_of_Slovnaft,_view_from_Nov%C3%BD_most_viewpoint_in_Bratislava,_Bratislava_II_District.jpg)

[Slovnaft](/wiki/Slovnaft "Slovnaft") oil refinery in Bratislava

[Slovenský plynárenský priemysel](/wiki/Slovensk%C3%BD_plyn%C3%A1rensk%C3%BD_priemysel "Slovenský plynárenský priemysel") (*Slovak Gas Industry*) is the biggest natural gas seller in Slovakia. In 2024, domestic gas production covers roughly 2% of Slovak consumption.[[221]](#cite_note-224) An average of around 65 million m³ of natural gas is produced, while 4,2 billion m³ of natural gas is imported.[[222]](#cite_note-225) As of 2025, most of gas imports to Slovakia is from Russia via [TurkStream](/wiki/TurkStream "TurkStream") pipeline. TurkStream connects Russia and Turkey, with gas flowing through the Balkans and Hungary before reaching Slovakia.[[223]](#cite_note-226)

The oil production in Slovakia is even lower than the gas production. In 2021 it reached a volume of 4,500 tons per year with a total oil consumption of around 6 million tons per year.[[224]](#cite_note-227) Among European Union countries, Slovakia is the most dependent on Russian oil and petroleum products.[[225]](#cite_note-228) In 2020, Russia was the origin country for 78 percent of the country's total oil imports. [Slovnaft](/wiki/Slovnaft "Slovnaft"), with 4,000 employees, is the largest oil refining company in Slovakia, located in Bratislava. The company refines 5.5 to 6 million tonnes of crude oil per annum and produces a broad range of motor fuels, fuel oils and petrochemical products.

### Transportation

Main article: [Transport in Slovakia](/wiki/Transport_in_Slovakia "Transport in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Strbske_Pleso_pociag_425_2.jpg/250px-Strbske_Pleso_pociag_425_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strbske_Pleso_pociag_425_2.jpg)

A train in the northern town of [Vysoké Tatry](/wiki/Vysok%C3%A9_Tatry "Vysoké Tatry")

There are four main motorway D1 to D4 and eight expressways R1 to R8. Many of them are still under construction.

The major motorway in Slovakia is [D1](/wiki/Motorway_D1_(Slovakia) "Motorway D1 (Slovakia)"), the motorway connects major cities across Slovakia from west to east, from Bratislava to [Trnava](/wiki/Trnava "Trnava"), [Nitra](/wiki/Nitra "Nitra"), [Trenčín](/wiki/Tren%C4%8D%C3%ADn "Trenčín"), [Žilina](/wiki/%C5%BDilina "Žilina") and beyond. As of 2025, some sections of D1 are still under construction. The [D2 motorway](/wiki/Motorway_D2_(Slovakia) "Motorway D2 (Slovakia)") connects it to [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno") and [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest") in the north–south direction. A large part of [D4 motorway](/wiki/Motorway_D4_(Slovakia) "Motorway D4 (Slovakia)") (an outer bypass), which ease the pressure on Bratislava's highway system, opened in 2021.[[226]](#cite_note-229) The [A6 motorway](/wiki/Nordostautobahn "Nordostautobahn") to [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") connects Slovakia directly to the Austrian motorway system and was opened on 19 November 2007.[[227]](#cite_note-230)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Dia%C4%BEnica_D1._Pre%C5%A1ov_z%C3%A1pad_21_Slovakia_13.jpg/250px-Dia%C4%BEnica_D1._Pre%C5%A1ov_z%C3%A1pad_21_Slovakia_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dia%C4%BEnica_D1._Pre%C5%A1ov_z%C3%A1pad_21_Slovakia_13.jpg)

[D1 motorway](/wiki/Motorway_D1_(Slovakia) "Motorway D1 (Slovakia)")

Slovakia has three international airports. [Bratislava Airport](/wiki/Bratislava_Airport "Bratislava Airport") is the main and largest [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport"). It is located 9 km northeast of the city centre. It serves civil and governmental, scheduled and unscheduled domestic and international flights. The current runways support the landing of all common types of aircraft currently used. The airport has enjoyed rapidly growing passenger traffic in recent years; it served 279,028 passengers in 2000 and 2,292,712 in 2018.[[228]](#cite_note-231) [Košice International Airport](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice_International_Airport "Košice International Airport") is an airport serving [Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice"). It is the second-largest [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") in Slovakia. The [Poprad–Tatry Airport](/wiki/Poprad%E2%80%93Tatry_Airport "Poprad–Tatry Airport") is the third busiest airport, the airport is located 5 km west-northwest of [Poprad](/wiki/Poprad "Poprad"). It is an airport with one of the highest elevations in Central Europe, at 718 m, which is 150 m higher than [Innsbruck Airport](/wiki/Innsbruck_Airport "Innsbruck Airport") in Austria.

[Railways of the Slovak Republic](/wiki/Railways_of_the_Slovak_Republic "Railways of the Slovak Republic") (*Železnice Slovenskej Republiky*) provides railway transport services on national and international lines.

The [Port of Bratislava](/wiki/Port_of_Bratislava "Port of Bratislava") is one of the two international [river ports](/wiki/Port "Port") in Slovakia. The port connects Bratislava to international boat traffic, especially the interconnection from the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") via the [Rhine-Main-Danube Canal](/wiki/Rhine%E2%80%93Main%E2%80%93Danube_Canal "Rhine–Main–Danube Canal").
Additionally, tourist boats operate from Bratislava's passenger port, including routes to [Devín](/wiki/Dev%C3%ADn "Devín"), [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") and elsewhere. The Port of Komárno is the second largest port in Slovakia with an area of over 20 hectares and is located approximately 100 km east of Bratislava. It lies at the confluence of two rivers – the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") and [Váh](/wiki/V%C3%A1h "Váh").

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Slovakia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Slovakia "Tourism in Slovakia")

See also: [List of castles in Slovakia](/wiki/List_of_castles_in_Slovakia "List of castles in Slovakia") and [List of World Heritage Sites in Slovakia](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Slovakia "List of World Heritage Sites in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Bojnice_Castle_Slovakia.jpg/250px-Bojnice_Castle_Slovakia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bojnice_Castle_Slovakia.jpg)

Bojnice Castle

Tourism is one of the main sectors of the Slovakia's economy, although still underserved. It is based on domestic tourism, as most of the tourists are the Slovak nationals and residents travelling for leisure within the country. Slovakia features natural landscapes, mountains, [caves](/wiki/List_of_caves_in_Slovakia "List of caves in Slovakia"), medieval [castles](/wiki/List_of_castles_in_Slovakia "List of castles in Slovakia") and towns, folk architecture, spas and [ski resorts](/wiki/Ski_and_winter_sports_in_Slovakia "Ski and winter sports in Slovakia"). More than 5,4 million tourists visited Slovakia in 2017. The most attractive destinations are the capital of [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava") and the [High Tatras](/wiki/High_Tatras "High Tatras").[[229]](#cite_note-232) Most visitors come from the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") (about 26%), Poland (15%) and Germany (11%).[[230]](#cite_note-233)

Slovakia contains many castles, most of which are in ruins. The best known castles include [Bojnice Castle](/wiki/Bojnice_Castle "Bojnice Castle") (often used as a filming location), [Spiš Castle](/wiki/Spi%C5%A1_Castle "Spiš Castle"), (on the UNESCO list), [Orava Castle](/wiki/Orava_Castle "Orava Castle"), [Bratislava Castle](/wiki/Bratislava_Castle "Bratislava Castle"), and the ruins of [Devín Castle](/wiki/Dev%C3%ADn_Castle "Devín Castle"). [Čachtice Castle](/wiki/%C4%8Cachtice_Castle "Čachtice Castle") was once the home of the world's most prolific female serial killer, the 'Bloody Lady', [Elizabeth Báthory](/wiki/Elizabeth_B%C3%A1thory "Elizabeth Báthory").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Bardejov%2C_rynek_%28HB1%29.jpg/250px-Bardejov%2C_rynek_%28HB1%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bardejov,_rynek_(HB1).jpg)

The centre of [Bardejov](/wiki/Bardejov "Bardejov") – a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")

Slovakia's position in Europe and the country's past (part of the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"), the [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg "Habsburg") monarchy and [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia")) made many cities and towns similar to the cities in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") (such as [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague")), Austria (such as [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg")) or Hungary (such as [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest")). A historical centre with at least one square has been preserved in many towns. Large historical centres can be found in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava"), [Trenčín](/wiki/Tren%C4%8D%C3%ADn "Trenčín"), [Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice"), [Banská Štiavnica](/wiki/Bansk%C3%A1_%C5%A0tiavnica "Banská Štiavnica"), [Levoča](/wiki/Levo%C4%8Da "Levoča"), and [Trnava](/wiki/Trnava "Trnava"). Historical centres have been going through a restoration in recent years.

Historical churches can be found in virtually every village and town in Slovakia. Most of them are built in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") style, but there are also many examples of [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") and [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture"), for example [Banská Bystrica](/wiki/Bansk%C3%A1_Bystrica "Banská Bystrica"), Bardejov and [Spišská Kapitula](/wiki/Spi%C5%A1sk%C3%A1_Kapitula "Spišská Kapitula"). The [Basilica of St. James](/wiki/Basilica_of_St._James,_Levo%C4%8Da "Basilica of St. James, Levoča") in Levoča with the tallest wood-carved altar in the world and the Church of the Holy Spirit in [Žehra](/wiki/%C5%BDehra "Žehra") with medieval [frescos](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") are UNESCO [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). The [St. Martin's Concathedral](/wiki/St._Martin%27s_Concathedral "St. Martin's Concathedral") in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava") served as the [coronation](/wiki/Coronation "Coronation") church for the Kingdom of Hungary. The oldest sacral buildings in Slovakia stem from the [Great Moravian](/wiki/Great_Moravia "Great Moravia") period in the ninth century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Jasn%C3%A1_Ski_Resort_-_gondola_lift_Kosodrevina_-_Chopok_%284%29.jpg/250px-Jasn%C3%A1_Ski_Resort_-_gondola_lift_Kosodrevina_-_Chopok_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jasn%C3%A1_Ski_Resort_-_gondola_lift_Kosodrevina_-_Chopok_(4).jpg)

Cable cars at [Jasná](/wiki/Jasn%C3%A1 "Jasná") in the Tatra Mountains

Very precious structures are the complete wooden churches of northern and northern-eastern Slovakia. Most were built from the 15th century onwards by [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") and members of [eastern-rite](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") churches.

There are many castles located throughout the country. Among the tourists, some of the most popular are [Bojnice Castle](/wiki/Bojnice_Castle "Bojnice Castle"), [Spiš Castle](/wiki/Spi%C5%A1_Castle "Spiš Castle"), [Stará Ľubovňa Castle](/wiki/%C4%BDubov%C5%88a_Castle "Ľubovňa Castle"), [Krásna Hôrka Castle](/wiki/Kr%C3%A1sna_H%C3%B4rka_Castle "Krásna Hôrka Castle"), [Orava Castle](/wiki/Orava_Castle "Orava Castle") (where many scenes of [Nosferatu](/wiki/Nosferatu "Nosferatu") were filmed), [Trenčín Castle](/wiki/Tren%C4%8D%C3%ADn_Castle "Trenčín Castle"), and [Bratislava Castle](/wiki/Bratislava_Castle "Bratislava Castle"), and also castles in ruins, such as [Beckov Castle](/wiki/Beckov_Castle "Beckov Castle"), [Devín Castle](/wiki/Dev%C3%ADn_Castle "Devín Castle"), [Šariš Castle](/wiki/%C5%A0ari%C5%A1_Castle "Šariš Castle"), [Považie Castle](/wiki/Pova%C5%BEsk%C3%BD_hrad "Považský hrad"), and [Strečno Castle](/wiki/Stre%C4%8Dno_Castle "Strečno Castle") (where [Dragonheart](/wiki/Dragonheart "Dragonheart") was filmed).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/20180503_Zamek_Spiski_2953_DxO.jpg/250px-20180503_Zamek_Spiski_2953_DxO.jpg)](/wiki/File:20180503_Zamek_Spiski_2953_DxO.jpg)

[Spiš Castle](/wiki/Spi%C5%A1_Castle "Spiš Castle")

Slovakia is also known for its numerous spas. [Piešťany](/wiki/Pie%C5%A1%C5%A5any "Piešťany") is the biggest and busiest spa town in the country, attracting many visitors from the [Gulf countries](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf"), mostly the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"), [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait"), and [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain"). [Bardejov](/wiki/Bardejov "Bardejov"), [Trenčianske Teplice](/wiki/Tren%C4%8Dianske_Teplice "Trenčianske Teplice"), [Turčianske Teplice](/wiki/Tur%C4%8Dianske_Teplice "Turčianske Teplice"), and [Rajecké Teplice](/wiki/Rajeck%C3%A9_Teplice "Rajecké Teplice") are other major spa towns. Some well-known minor spa towns and villages are [Štós](/wiki/%C5%A0t%C3%B3s "Štós"), [Číž](/wiki/%C4%8C%C3%AD%C5%BE "Číž"), [Dudince](/wiki/Dudince "Dudince"), [Kováčová](/wiki/Kov%C3%A1%C4%8Dov%C3%A1,_Zvolen_District "Kováčová, Zvolen District"), [Nimnica](/wiki/Nimnica "Nimnica"), [Smrdáky](/wiki/Smrd%C3%A1ky "Smrdáky"), [Lúčky](/wiki/L%C3%BA%C4%8Dky,_Ru%C5%BEomberok_District "Lúčky, Ružomberok District"), and [Vyšné Ružbachy](/wiki/Vy%C5%A1n%C3%A9_Ru%C5%BEbachy "Vyšné Ružbachy"), among others.

Typical souvenirs from Slovakia are dolls dressed in folk costumes, ceramic objects, crystal glass, carved wooden figures, črpáks (wooden pitchers), [fujaras](/wiki/Fujara "Fujara") (a [folk instrument](/wiki/Folk_instrument "Folk instrument") on the UNESCO list) and [valaškas](/wiki/Vala%C5%A1ka "Valaška") (a decorated folk hatchet) and above all products made from [corn husks](/wiki/Husk "Husk") and wire, notably human figures. Souvenirs can be bought in the shops run by the state organisation ÚĽUV (*Ústredie ľudovej umeleckej výroby*—Centre of [Folk Art](/wiki/Folk_art "Folk art") Production).

### Science

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/STANICA_LANOVKY_S_OBSERVAT%C3%93RIOM.JPG/250px-STANICA_LANOVKY_S_OBSERVAT%C3%93RIOM.JPG)](/wiki/File:STANICA_LANOVKY_S_OBSERVAT%C3%93RIOM.JPG)

[Observatory](/wiki/Observatory "Observatory") at the top of the peak [Lomnický štít](/wiki/Lomnick%C3%BD_%C5%A1t%C3%ADt "Lomnický štít"), at the altitude above sea level of 2,632 m (8,635 ft).

The [Slovak Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Slovak_Academy_of_Sciences "Slovak Academy of Sciences") has been the most important scientific and research institution in the country since 1953. Slovaks have made notable scientific and technical contributions during history.[[231]](#cite_note-234) In 1999, astronaut [Ivan Bella](/wiki/Ivan_Bella "Ivan Bella"), became the first and only Slovak citizen to fly in space.[[232]](#cite_note-235)

Observer status to [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") (ESA) was granted in 2010, when Slovakia signed the General Agreement on Cooperation in which information about ongoing education programmes was shared and Slovakia was invited to various negotiations of the ESA.[[233]](#cite_note-236) In 2015, Slovakia signed the European Cooperating State Agreement based on which Slovakia committed to the finance entrance programme named PECS (Plan for the European Cooperating States) which serves as preparation for full membership. Slovak research and development organisations can apply for funding of projects regarding space technologies advancement. Slovakia became European Space Agency associate member state in 2022.[[234]](#cite_note-www.esa.int-237) In 2024, the country signed the [Artemis Accords](/wiki/Artemis_Accords "Artemis Accords") with [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA").[[235]](#cite_note-238)

Slovakia was ranked 47th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[236]](#cite_note-239)[[237]](#cite_note-240)
