[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Population_density_in_Slovakia.png/250px-Population_density_in_Slovakia.png)](/wiki/File:Population_density_in_Slovakia.png)

Population density in Slovakia. The two biggest cities are clearly visible, Bratislava in the far west and Košice in the east.

The population is over 5.4 million and consists mostly of [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks"). The average population density is 110 inhabitants per km2.[[239]](#cite_note-242) According to the 2021 census, the majority of the inhabitants of Slovakia are [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks") (83.8%). [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians_in_Slovakia "Hungarians in Slovakia") are the largest [ethnic minority](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group") (7.8%). Other [ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") include [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") (1.2%),[[240]](#cite_note-243) [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") (0.5%), [Rusyns](/wiki/Rusyns "Rusyns") (0.4%) and others (1.9) or unspecified (5.4%).[[241]](#cite_note-244)

In 2024, the [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of the Slovak population was 42.8 years.[[242]](#cite_note-245)

The largest waves of Slovak emigration occurred in the 19th and early 20th centuries. In the 1990 US census, 1.8 million people self-identified as having Slovak ancestry.[[243]](#cite_note-246)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

In the 2024 Global Hunger Index, Slovakia is one of 22 countries with a GHI score of less than 5.[[244]](#cite_note-247)

### Languages

Main article: [Slovak language](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language")

See also: [History of the Slovak language](/wiki/History_of_the_Slovak_language "History of the Slovak language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Slovak_alphabet.png/250px-Slovak_alphabet.png)](/wiki/File:Slovak_alphabet.png)

The [Slovak alphabet](/wiki/Slovak_alphabet "Slovak alphabet") has 46 letters, of which 3 are [digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") and 18 contain [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic").

The [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") is [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"), a member of the [Slavic language family](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"). [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") is widely spoken in the southern regions, and [Rusyn](/wiki/Rusyn_language "Rusyn language") is used in some parts of the Northeast. [Minority languages](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") hold co-official status in the municipalities in which the size of the minority population meets the legal threshold of 15% in two consecutive censuses.[[245]](#cite_note-248)

Slovakia is ranked among the top EU countries regarding the knowledge of foreign languages. In 2007, 68% of the population aged from 25 to 64 years claimed to speak two or more foreign languages, finishing second highest in the European Union. The best known foreign language in Slovakia is [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"). [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat") report also shows that 98.3% of Slovak students in the upper secondary education take on two foreign languages, ranking highly over the average 60.1% in the European Union.[[246]](#cite_note-249) According to a [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") survey from 2012, 26% of the population have knowledge of English at a conversational level, followed by German (22%) and Russian (17%).[[247]](#cite_note-250)

The deaf community uses the [Slovak Sign Language](/wiki/Slovak_Sign_Language "Slovak Sign Language").

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Slovakia](/wiki/Religion_in_Slovakia "Religion in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Levo%C4%8Da_%282%29.jpg/250px-Levo%C4%8Da_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Levo%C4%8Da_(2).jpg)

Basilica of St. James in [Levoča](/wiki/Levo%C4%8Da "Levoča")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/D%C3%B3m_sv%C3%A4tej_Al%C5%BEbety_a_Kaplnka_sv._Michala%2C_Ko%C5%A1ice%2C_Slovensko.jpg/250px-D%C3%B3m_sv%C3%A4tej_Al%C5%BEbety_a_Kaplnka_sv._Michala%2C_Ko%C5%A1ice%2C_Slovensko.jpg)](/wiki/File:D%C3%B3m_sv%C3%A4tej_Al%C5%BEbety_a_Kaplnka_sv._Michala,_Ko%C5%A1ice,_Slovensko.jpg)

[St. Elisabeth Cathedral](/wiki/St._Elisabeth_Cathedral "St. Elisabeth Cathedral") in Košice, the largest church in Slovakia

The [Slovak constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovakia "Constitution of Slovakia") guarantees [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion"). In 2021, 55.8% of population identified themselves as [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Slovakia "Catholic Church in Slovakia") ([Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church")), 5.3% as [Lutherans](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_of_the_Augsburg_Confession_in_Slovakia "Evangelical Church of the Augsburg Confession in Slovakia"), 1.6% as [Calvinists](/wiki/Reformed_Christian_Church_in_Slovakia "Reformed Christian Church in Slovakia"), 4% as [Greek Catholics](/wiki/Slovak_Greek_Catholic_Church "Slovak Greek Catholic Church"), 0.9% as [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_the_Czech_Lands_and_Slovakia "Orthodox Church of the Czech Lands and Slovakia"), 23.8% identified themselves as [atheists](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") or non-religious, and 6.5% did not answer the question about their belief.[[248]](#cite_note-251) In 2004, about one third of the church members regularly attended church services.[[249]](#cite_note-252) The [Slovak Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Slovak_Greek_Catholic_Church "Slovak Greek Catholic Church") is an Eastern rite [sui iuris](/wiki/Sui_iuris "Sui iuris") Catholic Church. Before World War II, an estimated 90,000 Jews lived in Slovakia (1.6% of the population), but most were murdered during the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust"). After further reductions due to postwar [emigration](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah") and assimilation, only about 2,300 Jews remain today (0.04% of the population).[[250]](#cite_note-253)

There are 18 state-registered religions in Slovakia, of which 16 are Christian, one is Jewish, and one is the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith").[[251]](#cite_note-Hukelova-254) In 2016, a two-thirds majority of the Slovak parliament passed a new bill that would obstruct [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and other religious organisations from becoming state-recognised religions by doubling the minimum followers threshold from 25,000 to 50,000; however, Slovakia's then-president [Andrej Kiska](/wiki/Andrej_Kiska "Andrej Kiska") vetoed the bill.[[251]](#cite_note-Hukelova-254) In 2010, there were an estimated 5,000 [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Slovakia "Islam in Slovakia") in Slovakia representing less than 0.1% of the country's population.[[252]](#cite_note-255) Slovakia is the only member state of the European Union to not have any [mosques](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque").[[253]](#cite_note-256)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Slovakia](/wiki/Education_in_Slovakia "Education in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Univerzita_Komensk%C3%A9ho.jpg/250px-Univerzita_Komensk%C3%A9ho.jpg)](/wiki/File:Univerzita_Komensk%C3%A9ho.jpg)

[Comenius University](/wiki/Comenius_University_in_Bratislava "Comenius University in Bratislava") headquarters in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava")

Education in Slovakia is compulsory from age 6 to 16. Primary school in Slovakia lasts for nine years, which is divided into two parts, the first stage (age 6–10) and the second stage (age 10–15), which is finished by taking nationwide testing called *Monitor* (in Slovak language and math).

For most students, secondary education (high school) lasts four years, from ages 15 to 19. This is the typical length for students who complete the full nine years of primary school and then enroll in a secondary school. High school is finished by school-leaving exam called *Maturita*, or *Maturitná skúška*. The [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment"), coordinated by the [OECD](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), currently ranks [Slovak secondary education](/wiki/Education_in_Slovakia "Education in Slovakia") the 30th in the world (placing it just below the United States and just above Spain).[[254]](#cite_note-257)

After finishing high school, students can go to university. Slovakia has a wide range of universities. The biggest university is [Comenius University](/wiki/Comenius_University "Comenius University"), established in 1919. Although it is not the first university ever established on Slovak territory, it is the oldest university that is still running. Most universities in Slovakia are public funded, where anyone can apply. Every citizen has a right to free education in public schools. The country has also several privately funded universities. Anyone can apply to any number of universities.

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Slovakia](/wiki/Culture_of_Slovakia "Culture of Slovakia")

### Folk tradition

See also: [Music of Slovakia](/wiki/Music_of_Slovakia "Music of Slovakia") and [Architecture of Slovakia](/wiki/Architecture_of_Slovakia "Architecture of Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Vlkolinec_02.jpg/250px-Vlkolinec_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vlkolinec_02.jpg)

Wooden folk architecture can be seen in the well-preserved village of [Vlkolínec](/wiki/Vlkol%C3%ADnec "Vlkolínec"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").

[Folk tradition](/wiki/Folk_tradition "Folk tradition") has rooted strongly in Slovakia and is reflected in literature, music, dance and architecture. The prime example is the Slovak national anthem, *"[Nad Tatrou sa blýska](/wiki/Nad_Tatrou_sa_bl%C3%BDska "Nad Tatrou sa blýska")"*, which is based on a melody from the *"[Kopala studienku](/wiki/Kopala_studienku "Kopala studienku")"* folk song.

The manifestation of Slovak folklore culture is the "*Východná*" Folklore Festival. It is the oldest and largest nationwide festival with international participation,[[255]](#cite_note-258) which takes place in [Východná](/wiki/V%C3%BDchodn%C3%A1 "Východná") annually. Slovakia is usually represented by many groups but mainly by SĽUK (*Slovenský ľudový umelecký kolektív—Slovak folk art collective*). SĽUK is the largest Slovak folk art group, trying to preserve the folklore tradition.

An example of wooden folk architecture in Slovakia can be seen in the well-preserved village of [Vlkolínec](/wiki/Vlkol%C3%ADnec "Vlkolínec") which has been the [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") since 1993.[[256]](#cite_note-259) The [Prešov Region](/wiki/Pre%C5%A1ov_Region "Prešov Region") preserves the world's most remarkable folk wooden churches. Most of them are protected by Slovak law as [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage"), but some of them are on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") list too, in Bodružal, Hervartov, Ladomirová and Ruská Bystrá.

The best known Slovak hero, found in many folk [mythologies](/wiki/Mythologies "Mythologies"), is [Juraj Jánošík](/wiki/Juraj_J%C3%A1no%C5%A1%C3%ADk "Juraj Jánošík") (1688–1713) (the Slovak equivalent of [Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood "Robin Hood")). The legend says he was taking from the rich and giving to the poor. Jánošík's life was depicted in a list of literary works and many movies throughout the 20th century. One of the most popular is a film *Jánošík* directed by [Martin Frič](/wiki/Martin_Fri%C4%8D "Martin Frič") in 1935.[[257]](#cite_note-260) He is commemorated mainly in [Terchová](/wiki/Terchov%C3%A1 "Terchová"), his family's place of living.

### Art

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/St._James%2C_Levo%C4%8Da%2C_Main_altar%2C_2017_v2.jpg/250px-St._James%2C_Levo%C4%8Da%2C_Main_altar%2C_2017_v2.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._James,_Levo%C4%8Da,_Main_altar,_2017_v2.jpg)

Main altar in the Basilica of St. James, crafted by [Master Paul of Levoča](/wiki/Master_Paul_of_Levo%C4%8Da "Master Paul of Levoča"), 1517. It is the tallest wooden altar in the world

[Visual art](/wiki/Visual_art "Visual art") in Slovakia is represented through [painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting"), [drawing](/wiki/Drawing "Drawing"), [printmaking](/wiki/Printmaking "Printmaking"), [illustration](/wiki/Illustration "Illustration"), [arts and crafts](/wiki/Handicraft "Handicraft"), [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture"), [photography](/wiki/Photography "Photography") or [conceptual art](/wiki/Conceptual_art "Conceptual art"). The [Slovak National Gallery](/wiki/Slovak_National_Gallery "Slovak National Gallery") founded in 1948, is the biggest network of galleries in Slovakia. Two displays in Bratislava are situated in [Esterházy Palace](/wiki/Esterh%C3%A1zy_Palace_(Bratislava) "Esterházy Palace (Bratislava)") (*Esterházyho palác*) and the Water Barracks (*Vodné kasárne*), adjacent one to another. They are located on the Danube riverfront in the Old Town.[[258]](#cite_note-261)[[259]](#cite_note-262)

The [Bratislava City Gallery](/wiki/Bratislava_City_Gallery "Bratislava City Gallery"), founded in 1961 is the second biggest Slovak gallery of its kind. It stores about 35,000 pieces of Slovak international art and offers permanent displays in [Pálffy Palace](/wiki/Johann_P%C3%A1lffy_Palace "Johann Pálffy Palace") and [Mirbach Palace](/wiki/Mirbach_Palace "Mirbach Palace"), located in the Old Town. Danubiana Art Museum, one of the youngest art museums in Europe, is situated near [Čunovo](/wiki/%C4%8Cunovo "Čunovo") waterworks (part of [Gabčíkovo Waterworks](/wiki/Gab%C4%8D%C3%ADkovo_-_Nagymaros_Dams "Gabčíkovo - Nagymaros Dams")). Other major galleries include: [Andy Warhol Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Andy_Warhol_Museum_of_Modern_Art "Andy Warhol Museum of Modern Art") (Warhol's parents were from [Miková](/wiki/Mikov%C3%A1 "Miková")), [East Slovak Gallery](/wiki/East_Slovak_Gallery "East Slovak Gallery"), [Ernest Zmeták Art Gallery](/wiki/Ernest_Zmet%C3%A1k_Art_Gallery "Ernest Zmeták Art Gallery"), [Zvolen Castle](/wiki/Zvolen_Castle "Zvolen Castle").

### Literature

Further information: [Slovak literature](/wiki/Slovak_literature "Slovak literature")

See also: [List of Slovak authors](/wiki/List_of_Slovak_authors "List of Slovak authors")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Bozetech_Klemens_Stur.jpg/250px-Bozetech_Klemens_Stur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bozetech_Klemens_Stur.jpg)

[Ľudovít Štúr](/wiki/%C4%BDudov%C3%ADt_%C5%A0t%C3%BAr "Ľudovít Štúr") codified the Slovak standard language.

Christian topics include poem [Proglas](/wiki/Proglas "Proglas") as a foreword to the four [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel"), partial translations of the Bible into [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic"), *Zakon sudnyj ljudem*.

[Medieval literature](/wiki/Medieval_literature "Medieval literature"), in the period from the 11th to the 15th centuries, was written in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), Czech and Slovakised Czech. Lyric (prayers, songs and formulas) was still controlled by the Church, while epic was concentrated on legends. Authors from this period include [Johannes de Thurocz](/wiki/Johannes_de_Thurocz "Johannes de Thurocz"), author of the [Chronica Hungarorum](/wiki/Chronica_Hungarorum "Chronica Hungarorum") and Maurus, both of them Hungarians.[[260]](#cite_note-Phillips-263) The worldly literature also emerged and chronicles were written in this period.

Two leading persons codified Slovak. The first was [Anton Bernolák](/wiki/Anton_Bernol%C3%A1k "Anton Bernolák"), whose concept was based on the western Slovak [dialect](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") in 1787. It was the codification of the first-ever literary language of Slovaks. The second was [Ľudovít Štúr](/wiki/%C4%BDudov%C3%ADt_%C5%A0t%C3%BAr "Ľudovít Štúr"), whose formation of the Slovak took principles from the central Slovak dialect in 1843.

Slovakia is also known for its polyhistors, of whom include [Pavol Jozef Šafárik](/wiki/Pavel_Jozef_%C5%A0af%C3%A1rik "Pavel Jozef Šafárik"), [Matej Bel](/wiki/Matthias_Bel "Matthias Bel"), [Ján Kollár](/wiki/J%C3%A1n_Koll%C3%A1r "Ján Kollár"), and its political revolutionaries and reformists, such [Milan Rastislav Štefánik](/wiki/Milan_Rastislav_%C5%A0tef%C3%A1nik "Milan Rastislav Štefánik") and [Alexander Dubček](/wiki/Alexander_Dub%C4%8Dek "Alexander Dubček").

### Cuisine

Main article: [Slovak cuisine](/wiki/Slovak_cuisine "Slovak cuisine")

See also: [Slovak wine](/wiki/Slovak_wine "Slovak wine") and [Beer in Slovakia](/wiki/Beer_in_Slovakia "Beer in Slovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Star%C3%A1_Bystrica%2C_Brynzov%C3%A9_halu%C5%A1ky.jpg/250px-Star%C3%A1_Bystrica%2C_Brynzov%C3%A9_halu%C5%A1ky.jpg)](/wiki/File:Star%C3%A1_Bystrica,_Brynzov%C3%A9_halu%C5%A1ky.jpg)

[Bryndzové halušky](/wiki/Bryndzov%C3%A9_halu%C5%A1ky "Bryndzové halušky"), one of the [national dishes](/wiki/National_dish "National dish") in Slovakia

Traditional Slovak cuisine is based mainly on [pork](/wiki/Pork "Pork"), [poultry](/wiki/Poultry "Poultry") ([chicken](/wiki/Chicken "Chicken") is the most widely eaten, followed by [duck](/wiki/Duck "Duck"), [goose](/wiki/Goose "Goose"), and [turkey](/wiki/Turkey_as_food "Turkey as food")), [flour](/wiki/Flour "Flour"), [potatoes](/wiki/Potato "Potato"), [cabbage](/wiki/Cabbage "Cabbage"), and [milk products](/wiki/Milk_products "Milk products"). It is relatively closely related to [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_cuisine "Hungarian cuisine"), [Czech](/wiki/Czech_cuisine "Czech cuisine"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_cuisine "Polish cuisine") and [Austrian cuisine](/wiki/Austrian_cuisine "Austrian cuisine"). On the east it is also influenced by [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_cuisine "Ukrainian cuisine"), including [Lemko](/wiki/Lemkos "Lemkos") and [Rusyn](/wiki/Rusyns "Rusyns"). In comparison with other European countries, ["game meat"](/wiki/Game_(hunting) "Game (hunting)") is more accessible in Slovakia due to vast resources of forest and because hunting is relatively popular.[[261]](#cite_note-EuropeNations2000-264) [Boar](/wiki/Boar "Boar"), [rabbit](/wiki/Rabbit "Rabbit"), and [venison](/wiki/Venison "Venison") are generally available throughout the year. [Lamb](/wiki/Sheep "Sheep") and [goat](/wiki/Goat "Goat") are eaten but are not widely popular.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The traditional Slovak meals are [bryndzové halušky](/wiki/Bryndzov%C3%A9_halu%C5%A1ky "Bryndzové halušky"), bryndzové pirohy and other meals with potato dough and [bryndza](/wiki/Bryndza "Bryndza"). Bryndza is a salty cheese made of sheep milk, characterised by a strong taste and aroma. Bryndzové halušky especially is considered a national dish, and is very commonly found on the menu of traditional Slovak restaurants.

A typical soup is a [sauerkraut](/wiki/Sauerkraut "Sauerkraut") soup ("kapustnica"). A [blood sausage](/wiki/Black_pudding "Black pudding") called "krvavnica", made from any parts of a butchered pig, is also a specific Slovak meal.

Wine is enjoyed throughout Slovakia. [Slovak wine](/wiki/Slovak_wine "Slovak wine") comes predominantly from the southern areas along the Danube and its tributaries; the northern half of the country is too cold and mountainous to grow grapevines. Traditionally, white wine was more popular than red or rosé (except in some regions), and [sweet wine](/wiki/Sweetness_of_wine "Sweetness of wine") more popular than dry; however, in recent years tastes seem to be changing.[[262]](#cite_note-265) Beer (mainly of the [pilsener](/wiki/Pilsener "Pilsener") style, though [dark lagers](/wiki/Dunkel "Dunkel") are also consumed) is also popular.

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Slovakia](/wiki/Sport_in_Slovakia "Sport in Slovakia")

Sporting activities are practised widely in Slovakia, many of them on a professional level. [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") and [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") have traditionally been regarded as the most popular sports in Slovakia, though [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), [whitewater slalom](/wiki/Whitewater_slalom "Whitewater slalom"), [cycling](/wiki/Cycle_sport "Cycle sport"), [alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing"), [biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon "Biathlon") and [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics") are also popular.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Slovakia2010WinterOlympicscelebration2.jpg/250px-Slovakia2010WinterOlympicscelebration2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slovakia2010WinterOlympicscelebration2.jpg)

The [Slovak national ice hockey team](/wiki/Slovakia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Slovakia men's national ice hockey team") celebrating a victory against [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_national_men%27s_ice_hockey_team "Swedish national men's ice hockey team") at the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics")

One of the most popular team sports in Slovakia is [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"). Slovakia became a member of the [IIHF](/wiki/IIHF "IIHF") on 2 February 1993.[[263]](#cite_note-266) Since then, the team has won four medals in [Ice Hockey World Championships](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships"), consisting of one gold, two silver and a bronze. The most recent success was a silver medal at the [2012 IIHF World Championship](/wiki/2012_IIHF_World_Championship "2012 IIHF World Championship") in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"). The Slovak national hockey team made eight appearances in the [Olympic games](/wiki/Slovakia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Slovakia men's national ice hockey team"), finishing fourth in the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") and third with bronze medal at the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics") in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"). The country has 8,280 registered players and is ranked seventh in the [IIHF World Ranking](/wiki/IIHF_World_Ranking "IIHF World Ranking") at present. The Slovak hockey teams [HC Slovan Bratislava](/wiki/HC_Slovan_Bratislava "HC Slovan Bratislava") and [HC Lev Poprad](/wiki/HC_Lev_Poprad "HC Lev Poprad") participated in the [Kontinental Hockey League](/wiki/Kontinental_Hockey_League "Kontinental Hockey League").[[264]](#cite_note-267)

Slovakia hosted the [2011 IIHF World Championship](/wiki/2011_IIHF_World_Championship "2011 IIHF World Championship"), where [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team") won the gold medal and [2019 IIHF World Championship](/wiki/2019_IIHF_World_Championship "2019 IIHF World Championship"), where [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team") also won the gold medal. Both competitions took place in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava") and [Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice").[[265]](#cite_note-268)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Slovakia_bratislava_N%C3%A1rodn%C3%BD_futbalov%C3%BD_%C5%A1tadi%C3%B3n.jpg/250px-Slovakia_bratislava_N%C3%A1rodn%C3%BD_futbalov%C3%BD_%C5%A1tadi%C3%B3n.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slovakia_bratislava_N%C3%A1rodn%C3%BD_futbalov%C3%BD_%C5%A1tadi%C3%B3n.jpg)

[National football stadium](/wiki/Teheln%C3%A9_pole "Tehelné pole") in Bratislava

Football is the most popular sport in Slovakia, with over 400,000 registered players. Since 1993, the [Slovak national football team](/wiki/Slovak_national_football_team "Slovak national football team") has qualified for the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") once, in [2010](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup"). They progressed to the last 16, where they were defeated by the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team"). The most notable result was the 3–2 victory over [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"). In 2016, the [Slovak national football team](/wiki/Slovak_national_football_team "Slovak national football team") qualified for the [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") tournament, under head coach [Ján Kozák](/wiki/J%C3%A1n_Koz%C3%A1k_(footballer,_born_1954) "Ján Kozák (footballer, born 1954)"). This helped the team reach its best-ever position of 14th in the [FIFA World Rankings](/wiki/FIFA_World_Rankings "FIFA World Rankings").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In club competitions, only three teams have qualified for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") Group Stage, namely [MFK Košice](/wiki/MFK_Ko%C5%A1ice "MFK Košice") in [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_UEFA_Champions_League "1997–98 UEFA Champions League"), [FC Artmedia Bratislava](/wiki/FC_Artmedia_Bratislava "FC Artmedia Bratislava") in [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_UEFA_Champions_League "2005–06 UEFA Champions League"), and [MŠK Žilina](/wiki/M%C5%A0K_%C5%BDilina "MŠK Žilina") in [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_UEFA_Champions_League "2010–11 UEFA Champions League"). FC Artmedia Bratislava has been the most successful team, finishing third at the Group Stage of the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_UEFA_Cup "2005–06 UEFA Cup"), therefore qualifying for the knockout stage. They remain the only Slovak club that has won a match at the group stage.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Bibliography

* Julius Bartl et al., *Slovak History: Chronology & Lexicon.* Wauconda, IL: Bolchazy-Carducci Publishers, 2002.
* Olga Drobna, Eduard Drobny, and Magdalena Gocnikova, *Slovakia: The Heart of Europe.* Wauconda, IL: Bolchazy-Carducci Publishers, 1996.
* Pavel Dvorak, *The Early History of Slovakia in Images.* Budmerice, Slovakia: Vydavatel'stvo Rak Budmerice, 2006.
* Sharon Fisher, *Political Change in Post-Communist Slovakia and Croatia: From Nationalist to Europeanist.* New York: Palgrave Macmillan, 2006.
* Karen Henderson, *Slovakia: The Escape from Invisibility.* London: Routledge, 2002.
* Rob Humphrey, *The Rough Guide to the Czech and Slovak Republics.* New York: Rough Guides, 2006.
* Michael Jacobs, *Blue Guide: Czech and Slovak Republics.* London: A.&C. Black, 1999.
* Owen V. Johnson, *Slovakia 1918–1938: Education and the Making of a Nation.* Boulder, CO: East European Monographs, 1985.
* Lil Junas, *My Slovakia: An American's View.* Martin, Slovakia: Vydavatelśtvo Matice slovenskej, 2001.
* Stanislav Kirschbaum, *A History of Slovakia: The Struggle for Survival.* New York: St. Martin's Press, 1995.
* Eugen Lazistan, Fedor Mikovič, Ivan Kučma, and Anna Jurečková, *Slovakia: A Photographic Odyssey.* Wauconda, IL: Bolchazy-Carducci Publishers, 2001.
* Elena Mannová, *A Concise History of Slovakia.* Bratislava: Historický ústav SAV, 2000.
* Anton Spiesz and Dusan Caplovic, *Illustrated Slovak History: A Struggle for Sovereignty in Central Europe.* Wauconda, IL : Bolchazy-Carducci Publishers, 2001.
